proposals.title: Meta-Select: Task-Adaptive Composition of Relational Priors via a Learned Repertoire
proposals.motivation: Modern architectures like Transformers rely on a monolithic self-attention mechanism to model relations, which may be suboptimal as different tasks (e.g., spatial reasoning vs. logical inference) require different relational priors. This one-size-fits-all approach limits both sample efficiency and generalization. A method to adapt the core relational computation to the task context, inspired by efficient adaptive systems, is a critical open problem in meta-learning and efficient transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned system that maintains a diverse repertoire of simple, specialized relation networks and dynamically composes them based on initial task observations will achieve superior task adaptation and data efficiency compared to a fixed relational encoder or a single network trained from scratch.
proposals.method: We will pre-train a diverse repertoire of lightweight relation networks (e.g., small attention variants, linear transformers, or graph networks) on a broad distribution of synthetic relational tasks. A meta-controller, implemented as a small hypernetwork or a per-task attention mechanism over the repertoire, will be meta-trained using gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML or Reptile) to generate task-specific weights or a selection mask from a few support examples. The selected composition will then process the query set, with the entire system optimized end-to-end for fast adaptation. We will leverage asynchronous and distributed training techniques (inspired by Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo) for scalable repertoire pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for task-adaptive relational computation, moving beyond fixed architectural biases. It will provide a practical framework for rapid adaptation in few-shot learning scenarios and contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the flexibility of neural architectures. The resulting method should demonstrate improved performance and efficiency on a diverse suite of reasoning and language understanding tasks.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is framing relation network selection as a meta-learning problem over a learned, composable repertoire, drawing a direct analogy to adaptive immune system selection. This differs from prior work on modular networks or mixture-of-experts by focusing on the fundamental relational operation and enabling rapid, context-driven architectural specialization at inference time.

proposals.title: Disentangled Conditional Information Bottleneck for Robust and Efficient Transfer
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models, especially large language and multimodal models, are brittle to distribution shifts and adversarial perturbations, limiting their reliable deployment. The classic Information Bottleneck (IB) principle offers a theoretical foundation for learning compressed representations but often discards information indiscriminately, harming performance on downstream tasks. A method to explicitly disentangle and control task-relevant and nuisance information in latent spaces is needed for robust generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: By structuring the encoder to produce separate latent variables for task-relevant and task-irrelevant information and applying a bottleneck penalty only to the irrelevant component, we can learn representations that are maximally informative for the target task while being invariant to specified nuisance factors, leading to superior robustness and sample efficiency in transfer learning.
proposals.method: We propose a novel encoder architecture with parallel pathways, inspired by disentanglement literature, to output structured latents (z_rel, z_irr). The training objective combines a standard predictive loss on z_rel with a conditional IB penalty that minimizes the mutual information between the input and z_irr, conditioned on the task label. We will leverage asynchronous local training schemes (like Async Local-SGD) for scalable optimization and evaluate on domain generalization benchmarks (e.g., DomainBed) and adversarial robustness tasks, measuring both performance and the degree of disentanglement achieved.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, theoretically grounded framework for learning robust latent representations that explicitly enforce invariance. It will yield practical benefits for domain generalization and fair AI by offering better control over encoded information. The integration with efficient distributed training methods will demonstrate its scalability to large-scale models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard IB or adversarial invariance methods, our approach is conditional and asymmetric, applying compression only to the nuisance pathway, which is a non-obvious but critical design for preserving predictive power. It uniquely combines ideas from disentanglement, information theory, and efficient distributed training (Local-SGD) for scalable robust representation learning.

proposals.title: ThermoBottleneck: A Fluctuation-Theoretic Objective for Compressed and Robust Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: The Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) provides a powerful framework for learning compressed, task-relevant representations, but its Lagrangian objective can be sensitive to the choice of the trade-off parameter β. Drawing a principled analogy from statistical thermodynamics, where optimal work extraction balances output against entropic dissipation governed by fluctuation theorems, could yield a more grounded and potentially self-tuning regularization strategy for representation learning, especially in large-scale, multi-task settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Reformulating the VIB objective through the lens of thermodynamic work extraction and non-equilibrium fluctuation theorems (e.g., Jarzynski equality) will enforce a stricter, more physically-inspired bottleneck, leading to latent representations that are more compressed and transferable across related tasks without manual β-tuning.
proposals.method: We will derive a novel training objective by treating the maximization of task-relevant information (I(Z;Y)) as 'useful work' and the compression cost (I(Z;X)) as 'dissipation'. This leads to a loss function based on the exponential average from fluctuation theorems, L = -log E[exp(-T * L_task)], where L_task is the task loss and T a 'temperature' parameter. We will integrate this objective into the training of encoder networks within large language model backbones (e.g., Gemini-style architectures), leveraging asynchronous local-SGD techniques for stable distributed training and evaluating transfer performance across task families in a DiPaCo-like distributed path composition setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, thermodynamically-grounded objective for the Information Bottleneck that may auto-balance the compression-prediction trade-off. 2) Empirical evidence on whether this leads to more robust and transferable representations in large-scale multimodal and language tasks, advancing the frontier of efficient model training and adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While connections between information theory and thermodynamics are known, directly applying non-equilibrium work-extraction principles and fluctuation theorems to derive a practical, alternative VIB training objective is novel and has not been explored in deep learning.

proposals.title: Flow-VIB: Task-Aware Compression via Expressive Latent Flows and the Variational Information Bottleneck
proposals.motivation: The Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) provides a principled framework for learning compressed, robust representations, but its standard Gaussian prior limits its expressiveness for complex, real-world data distributions like images or speech. Concurrently, normalizing flows offer powerful, invertible density estimation but lack a direct objective for discarding nuisance information. Bridging these methods could yield a highly flexible bottleneck for superior compression-performance trade-offs in multi-task and transfer learning settings, which are central to scaling modern AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regularizing a normalizing flow's latent space with the VIB objective will create a more expressive, non-Gaussian bottleneck that learns compressed representations which are significantly more robust and task-efficient than those from a standard Gaussian VIB or a flow model alone.
proposals.method: We will construct an encoder-decoder architecture where the encoder maps input data to the base distribution of a normalizing flow (e.g., RealNVP or Glow). The VIB objective, I(Z;X) - βI(Z;Y), will be applied directly to this expressive latent variable Z, with the KL divergence term computed using the flow's tractable log-likelihood. The decoder (task head) will be trained on Z to predict the target Y. We will evaluate this 'Flow-VIB' on multi-task benchmarks and in a transfer learning setup inspired by the researcher's work on DiPaCo, where compressed, task-aware representations are crucial for efficient distributed composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for learning expressive, compressed representations, demonstrating improved trade-off curves between compression rate, robustness to adversarial or noisy inputs, and downstream task accuracy. It will provide a new tool for creating efficient, transferable modules suitable for large-scale, distributed model architectures.
proposals.novelty: While VIB and flows are established independently, their integration to form a non-Gaussian, information-theoretic bottleneck is novel. The key innovation is using the flow not just for density estimation, but as the core latent space that is directly regularized by the information bottleneck to be maximally compressive yet task-relevant.

proposals.title: Adaptive Hypernetworks: A Closed-Loop Control System for Dynamic Parameter Generation
proposals.motivation: Hypernetworks offer a powerful paradigm for conditional parameter generation, enabling efficient multi-task learning and model personalization. However, existing approaches typically generate parameters in a single forward pass based on a static context, lacking the ability to adapt to the dynamic, non-stationary state of the primary network's training process. This limits their efficiency and robustness, particularly for complex, long-horizon tasks or in non-IID distributed settings like those explored in Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing hypernetwork parameter generation as an adaptive control problem, where the hypernetwork acts as a controller that continuously adjusts its outputs based on real-time feedback from the primary network, will lead to faster convergence, improved learning stability, and better final performance compared to static conditional generation.
proposals.method: We will design a hypernetwork architecture that takes as input both a task/module embedding and a dynamic feedback signal, such as a compressed history of recent gradients or layer-wise feature map statistics from the primary network. This feedback will be processed by a recurrent or transformer-based controller within the hypernetwork. The system will be trained end-to-end, with the hypernetwork's parameters updated to minimize the primary network's loss, creating a meta-learning loop. We will evaluate this on large-scale language modeling benchmarks, drawing on expertise from Gemini-scale models, and in distributed training scenarios to assess communication efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, control-theoretic perspective to hypernetwork design, moving from open-loop to closed-loop parameter synthesis. It will provide a method for more sample-efficient and stable training of large conditional models. The resulting framework could reduce the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning and improve performance in federated or continual learning settings by allowing local models to adapt more intelligently.
proposals.novelty: While hypernetworks and meta-learning are established, the explicit analogy to adaptive control systems and the integration of a dynamic, training-state feedback loop into the parameter generation process is a novel architectural and conceptual departure. This transforms the hypernetwork from a static conditioner into an active, responsive controller.

proposals.title: Amortized Variational Inference for Efficient and Robust Multi-Task Transfer in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale models like Gemini excel at individual tasks but face challenges in efficient, robust multi-task learning and continual adaptation. Pre-2026, methods like DiPaCo explore distributed composition, but principled uncertainty quantification and task-specific latent structure are often underutilized. Integrating Bayesian deep learning's amortized inference can provide a unified framework for learning reusable, task-agnostic representations while enabling efficient, data-sparse adaptation to new tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: That employing advanced amortized variational inference to model the latent posterior over task-specific parameters will yield more sample-efficient, robust, and compositionally generalizable multi-task learners compared to standard fine-tuning or prompt-based methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo framework by introducing a variational module that amortizes inference over task descriptors. For each task in a stream, a hypernetwork (inspired by FiLM or conditional batch norm) will generate task-specific parameters from a latent code, with the posterior regularized by a prior over task families. Training will use local-SGD for stability across asynchronous workers, with the ELBO objective encouraging disentangled, reusable latent factors. We will benchmark on a diverse set of reasoning and agentic tasks from the Gemini 2.5 evaluation suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable Bayesian method for multi-task and continual learning in LLMs, reducing catastrophic forgetting and improving few-shot adaptation. It will contribute a new, modular architecture for distributed path composition that explicitly models uncertainty and task relationships, advancing beyond purely deterministic merging strategies.
proposals.novelty: The integration of amortized variational inference into a large-scale, asynchronously trained model composition framework (DiPaCo) is novel, moving beyond point estimates to probabilistic task representations. This combines the efficiency of hypernetworks with the robustness of Bayesian principles for agentic and multimodal task transfer.

proposals.title: LaNAS: Latent Neural Architecture Search for Few-Shot Transfer
proposals.motivation: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is computationally prohibitive, requiring thousands of architecture evaluations. This bottleneck is especially severe for few-shot optimization on new tasks, where rapid adaptation is crucial. A method to search efficiently in a compressed, semantically meaningful space of architectures would drastically reduce the cost and time of NAS, enabling its practical application in dynamic, multi-task settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous latent space of neural architectures, learned from a diverse set of pre-trained models, can serve as an efficient and high-quality search space for few-shot architecture optimization, outperforming traditional discrete or cell-based NAS methods in sample efficiency.
proposals.method: We will first train a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) on a large corpus of diverse, high-performing neural network architectures (e.g., from NAS-Bench, Transformer variants, and ConvNet families) to learn a continuous latent representation. For a new target task, we will perform gradient-based optimization (e.g., using REINFORCE or a learned performance predictor) within this latent space, guided by a few-shot performance signal. The latent codes are then decoded into full architectures for final training and evaluation, leveraging asynchronous local-SGD techniques for efficient distributed training of candidate models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, sample-efficient paradigm for NAS that is tailored for rapid adaptation. It will provide a method to perform architecture search orders of magnitude faster than conventional NAS, directly applicable to multi-task and transfer learning pipelines. We will release the pre-trained architecture VAE and a benchmark for latent-space NAS.
proposals.novelty: While latent spaces are common for data, applying them directly as the search space for neural architectures is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts NAS from discrete graph operations to continuous optimization, combining the representational power of generative models with the efficiency of few-shot learning, a synergy not explored in pre-2026 NAS literature.

proposals.title: Dynamic Subspace Composition: Ultra-Low-Dimensional Adaptation via Sparse Routing
proposals.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA use low-rank adaptations, but their dimensionality (often 8+) still creates opaque, entangled representations that hinder interpretability. Ultra-low-dimensional (2-3D) subspaces offer maximal interpretability and minimal parameter overhead, but a single such subspace is catastrophically limiting for complex tasks. This work addresses the core tension between interpretability and expressivity in model adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A compositional system of many ultra-low-dimensional (2-3D) latent subspaces, dynamically selected and combined per input by a lightweight router, can achieve the expressivity needed for complex adaptation while preserving the interpretability and parameter efficiency of individual low-dimensional components.
proposals.method: We will train a large set of independent, ultra-low-rank (rank 2-3) adaptation matrices, each defining a minimal concept subspace. A small, sparsely-gated router network (inspired by mixture-of-experts) will process input embeddings to produce a sparse composition vector. For a given input, the final adaptation is the sparse sum of the selected 2-3D matrices, dynamically constructing a task-specific projection. Training will use an asynchronous local-SGD framework (as in Rusu's prior work) to distribute the learning of subspaces and the router across workers, with a sparsity-inducing loss on the router's outputs.
proposals.contribution: This introduces a novel PEFT paradigm that bridges the gap between highly interpretable linear probes and high-performance adapters. It provides a framework for analyzing model adaptations as compositions of elementary concepts. The method promises extreme parameter efficiency for edge deployment and a new lens for mechanistic interpretability of fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard LoRA or its multi-branch variants, this method enforces an ultra-low rank (2-3) per component and relies on dynamic, input-dependent composition to regain expressivity, creating a sparse, modular 'vocabulary' of adaptations rather than a single dense update.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Tasks and Adaptation Dynamics from Parameter Trajectories via Bayesian Meta-Inference
proposals.motivation: Understanding what a model has learned or adapted to from its parameters is a fundamental inverse problem in machine learning, crucial for interpretability, security, and knowledge transfer. Current methods, like task vectors or linear probes, rely on static parameter snapshots and assume a known task structure, failing to leverage the rich information in the full adaptation dynamics. This limits our ability to reverse-engineer learning processes in black-box settings or from distributed training logs, such as those from DiPaCo or asynchronous Local-SGD runs.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the full trajectory of parameter changes during adaptation with a structured prior over a compositional task space, we can jointly and robustly infer the latent task description and the adaptation process characteristics (e.g., optimizer, hyperparameters) that generated the observed dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian meta-inference framework where the observed parameter trajectory from a base model (e.g., a Gemini variant) is compared against simulated trajectories generated by a known learner model (e.g., a low-precision optimizer like Flash). The task space will be defined by a grammar over known capabilities (e.g., from the Gemini 2.5 evaluation suite). Inference will use amortized variational methods or sequential Monte Carlo to approximate the posterior over task descriptions and adaptation hyperparameters, efficiently searching the compositional space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for the meta-inverse problem, yielding interpretable posterior distributions over what was learned and how. It will advance the analysis of distributed and continual training systems, enabling new diagnostics for asynchronous training (like Local-SGD) and safer knowledge transfer by verifying the provenance of adapted models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior inverse problem approaches that use only final parameters, our method uniquely leverages the full temporal dynamics of adaptation. Furthermore, it jointly infers both the task and the adaptation process parameters, which is a significantly more general and ill-posed problem than standard task identification.

proposals.title: Inverse Task Identification: Decoding Task Semantics from Parameter Deltas in Adapted Models
proposals.motivation: Understanding what a model learns during fine-tuning or parameter-efficient adaptation (e.g., LoRA) is a black box. Current methods rely on probing outputs, not the adaptation process itself. This work aims to directly decode the semantic content of a new task from the structural changes (Δθ) in the model's parameters, enabling interpretable diagnostics of model adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: The parameter deltas (Δθ) induced by adapting a pre-trained model to a task contain structured, decodable information about the semantics of that task, allowing a learned decoder to infer a task description from Δθ alone.
proposals.method: We will curate a large-scale dataset of adaptation episodes on a frozen, pre-trained model (e.g., Gemini). Each episode involves adapting the model to a distinct task via a method like LoRA, producing a Δθ, and pairing it with a ground-truth task representation (e.g., a text embedding of the instruction). We will train a transformer-based 'task decoder' network that takes a compressed, structured representation of the sparse Δθ as input and outputs a predicted task embedding. Techniques from Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo will inform efficient, distributed training of this decoder across many episodes.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for task-introspection in neural networks via inverse identification. 2) A method to compute task similarity in parameter-change space, enabling taxonomy of learned skills. 3) Potential applications in guiding few-shot adaptation and detecting task interference or catastrophic forgetting during continual learning.
proposals.novelty: While analyzing weight changes is known, framing it as an inverse system identification problem to decode explicit task semantics is novel. The approach moves beyond correlating performance with Δθ to actively reconstructing a description of the 'disturbance' (task) applied to the model.

proposals.title: TaskVec: Inverting Parameter Updates to Recover Latent Task Descriptors
proposals.motivation: Modern transfer learning and fine-tuning adapt large pre-trained models to specific tasks, but the resulting parameter changes are treated as opaque. Understanding what these changes encode about the target task is a fundamental inverse problem. Solving it would enable task similarity analysis, efficient multi-task composition, and explainable adaptation, directly addressing challenges in scaling and interpreting models like Gemini.
proposals.hypothesis: The low-dimensional subspace of parameter changes induced by task-specific adaptation contains a recoverable, structured representation of the latent task identity. This representation can be decoded to generate a semantic description of the task.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DiPaCo framework to generate a diverse dataset of parameter deltas from a large pre-trained model (e.g., a Gemini variant) adapted to many tasks. We will train an inverse mapping network, inspired by hypernetworks, that takes a parameter delta as input and outputs a fixed-dimensional 'Task Vector'. This vector will be aligned with a text-based task descriptor using a contrastive loss, similar to CLIP, trained on natural language task descriptions. We will analyze the geometry of this latent space for task relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a method to explicitly characterize tasks via their parameter footprints, providing a new lens for task taxonomy and model interpretability. It will yield a practical tool for predicting task compatibility and conflict, informing more efficient multi-task and continual learning strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses parameter differences for model merging or editing, we propose to directly invert them into a human-interpretable latent space aligned with language. This moves beyond using deltas as mere weights to treating them as encodings of task semantics.

proposals.title: EcoLEO: Hierarchical Meta-Learning for Many-Shot Adaptation via Ecological Succession Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Meta-learning frameworks like LEO excel in few-shot learning but struggle to scale efficiently to many-shot regimes where abundant per-task data is available. This inefficiency limits their application to real-world, data-rich tasks. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where pioneer species facilitate complex ecosystem development, offers a novel blueprint for structuring this scaling process.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hierarchical meta-learner, structured to mimic the staged, facilitative process of ecological succession, will enable more parameter-efficient, stable, and performant adaptation to many-shot tasks compared to monolithic fine-tuning or naive scaling of existing meta-learning algorithms.
proposals.method: We will redesign the LEO adaptation mechanism into a multi-phase hierarchical process. A lightweight 'pioneer' module (e.g., a small adapter) performs initial, rapid task identification. Its output conditions a larger 'intermediate' module for feature refinement, which in turn conditions a final 'climax' module for high-precision specialization. Training will use a distributed, asynchronous curriculum (inspired by DiPaCo/Async Local-SGD) where tasks are sampled with varying data volumes, and a meta-loss supervises the entire succession chain, enforcing that each stage facilitates the next.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, biologically-inspired algorithm for scalable meta-learning that bridges the few-shot to many-shot gap. It will yield empirical results on large-scale multimodal benchmarks (extending Gemini-style models) and a theoretical framework for hierarchical, facilitative adaptation in neural networks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit imposition of a staged, ecological succession structure onto the inner-loop adaptation process of meta-learning, moving beyond uniform fine-tuning or simple architectural scaling. This represents a novel cross-disciplinary application of ecological principles to deep learning optimization.

proposals.title: LEO-Many: Scaling Latent Embedding Optimization for Data-Rich Task Adaptation
proposals.motivation: The LEO framework (Rusu et al., 2019) elegantly enables few-shot meta-learning by learning a generative model of initial parameters in a low-dimensional latent space. However, its design for data-scarce scenarios limits its application to real-world problems where adaptation data is abundant (e.g., fine-tuning on a large proprietary dataset). The field lacks a principled method to leverage meta-learning's efficiency and generalization in such many-shot regimes, where naive fine-tuning can be computationally expensive and prone to catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the LEO framework with mechanisms for progressive latent space refinement and dynamic capacity allocation, we can achieve superior data-efficient adaptation and final-task performance in many-shot scenarios compared to standard fine-tuning and other meta-learning baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the LEO framework by first pre-training its encoder and decoder on a diverse meta-training set. For many-shot adaptation, we will replace the few-shot inner-loop with a scalable, local training procedure like Asynchronous Local-SGD, operating within the latent space. The latent code will be iteratively refined over multiple communication rounds, allowing the model to compose knowledge from distributed paths (inspired by DiPaCo). We will integrate a gating mechanism, conditioned on task data, to dynamically blend the meta-learned prior with task-specific updates, preventing overfitting.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the applicability of gradient-based meta-learning to data-rich domains, providing a unified framework for both few-shot and many-shot adaptation. It will deliver a method that is more parameter-efficient and robust than direct fine-tuning, validated on large-scale language and multimodal tasks using models like Gemini.
proposals.novelty: While LEO is designed for few-shot learning, its extension to many-shot adaptation via distributed, latent-space fine-tuning is unexplored. The novel integration of scalable local training algorithms and dynamic path composition within a latent embedding optimization framework is a non-obvious and significant advancement.

proposals.title: Policy Distillation via Interpretable Path Composition (PD-IPC): Extracting Modular Programs from Neural Policies
proposals.motivation: Current policy distillation methods produce another opaque neural network, limiting interpretability, formal verification, and efficient deployment in specialized hardware. As agentic capabilities in models like Gemini advance, understanding and trusting their decision-making becomes critical for real-world applications. A distillation process yielding a modular, symbolic representation would address these limitations, enabling safer and more reliable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex, monolithic policy of a large-scale neural agent (e.g., a Gemini-based model) can be faithfully distilled into a collection of interpretable, composable functional modules, represented as a computational graph or program, without catastrophic performance loss on the target task distribution.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework, treating learned policy components as 'paths' within a modular architecture. Using a two-stage process, we first train a large, monolithic teacher policy (e.g., a Flash-trained Transformer). Second, we perform knowledge distillation into a DiPaCo-style sparse mixture-of-experts model, where each expert is constrained to be a small, interpretable function (e.g., a decision tree, linear model, or symbolic program). We will employ Asynchronous Local-SGD for efficient, distributed training of this modular student, using trajectory data from the teacher for supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for extracting interpretable, modular policies from state-of-the-art neural agents, bridging the gap between high performance and transparency. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the fidelity of symbolic policy distillation and demonstrate the utility of the DiPaCo paradigm for model interpretability, not just efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While policy distillation is well-studied, distilling into a *non-neural*, modular symbolic representation using a path-composition framework like DiPaCo is novel. The approach uniquely combines advanced distributed training (Local-SGD) with structured sparsity to induce interpretability, moving beyond distilling into a smaller black-box network.

proposals.title: Robust Policy Distillation via Adversarial Environment Generation
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a core technique for transferring capabilities from large, powerful models (e.g., Gemini) to smaller, more efficient agents. However, distilled policies are brittle and often fail under distributional shift, such as encountering novel task variations or adversarial perturbations at deployment. This limits the reliability of compact, high-performance agents in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a generative adversarial network (GAN) framework into the distillation process, where a generator creates challenging environmental variations and a distilled student policy is trained to be robust against them, will yield a policy with significantly improved generalization and robustness compared to standard distillation.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training loop building on Asynchronous Local-SGD for stable, distributed training. A generator network, conditioned on a base task, produces parameters for environmental perturbations. This generator and the student policy are trained adversarially: the generator aims to maximize the student's loss (creating hard examples), while the student, distilled from a fixed, powerful teacher (e.g., a Gemini-based agent), minimizes its loss on these adversarial examples. The training will utilize a distributed, path-composition style framework akin to DiPaCo to manage the multi-component optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new method for robust policy distillation that explicitly optimizes for out-of-distribution performance. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark suites for distributional shift in RL and agent tasks, demonstrating improved robustness. The resulting framework will be a step towards more reliable and deployable distilled agents.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and distillation are well-studied separately, their integration for policy robustness via *adversarial environment generation* is novel. This approach moves beyond simple input perturbations, instead challenging the policy's core decision-making logic with dynamically generated, semantically meaningful environmental shifts during distillation.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-Distill: Efficient Cross-Domain Transfer via Distributed Policy Distillation
proposals.motivation: Transfer learning is crucial for adapting large, pre-trained models to new tasks, but direct fine-tuning can be inefficient and prone to catastrophic forgetting, especially across distinct but related domains (e.g., code generation to mathematical reasoning). Pre-2026, distillation has proven effective for model compression and specialization, but its systematic use as a flexible, scalable bridge for cross-domain transfer within distributed training frameworks remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that policy distillation, applied within a distributed path composition framework like DiPaCo, can create a more parameter-efficient and stable transfer mechanism between related task domains than sequential fine-tuning, by preserving and transferring high-level policy abstractions rather than low-level feature mappings.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to incorporate asynchronous distillation objectives. A large, pre-trained 'teacher' model (e.g., a variant of Gemini) specialized on a source domain will guide the training of a 'student' model initialized for a target domain. The training will use a local-SGD regime across distributed workers, where each worker computes a combination of the local task loss and a Kullback-Leibler divergence loss from the teacher's policy. The DiPaCo router will learn to blend expert pathways, with distillation acting as a regularizer to align the student's latent representations and output distributions with the teacher's abstract problem-solving policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable method for cross-domain transfer that reduces forgetting and improves sample efficiency. It will demonstrate the integration of distillation as a core primitive within next-generation distributed training systems, providing empirical results on transfer between complex domains like code-to-math or vision-to-robotics. The outcome will be a more composable and efficient paradigm for leveraging existing model capabilities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing distillation not just for compression or single-task imitation, but as a foundational, distributed mechanism for continual and multi-domain learning within a path-composition model architecture, moving beyond simple parameter fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Inverse Distillation: Accelerating Large Model Training via Policy Expansion
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a standard technique for compressing large models into smaller, efficient ones. However, the inverse process—using a small, distilled policy to seed and accelerate the training of a larger, more powerful network—remains largely unexplored. This inverse problem is critical for efficient large-scale model development, as it could drastically reduce the immense computational cost and time required to train frontier models like Gemini, enabling faster iteration cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small, high-performance distilled policy, which has internalized robust behavioral priors, can serve as an effective initialization and training guide for a larger model, leading to faster convergence and superior final performance compared to training from scratch or standard initialization.
proposals.method: We will leverage a distilled, high-performance 'seed' policy from a model like Gemini 2.5. This seed will be used to initialize a subset of layers in a larger, randomly initialized target network via function-preserving expansion techniques. We will then employ a modified Asynchronous Local-SGD training framework, where the seed policy acts as a consistent, high-quality local guide, providing soft targets and auxiliary losses. The DiPaCo framework will be adapted to orchestrate this asymmetric, guided training across a distributed system, efficiently scaling the expansion process.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm for model scaling, demonstrating that high-quality small models are not just endpoints but valuable seeds for larger ones. It will provide a concrete method to significantly reduce the wall-clock time and FLOPs required to train state-of-the-art large language models. The resulting 'expansion' algorithms and distributed training strategy will be a direct contribution to the efficiency-focused ML community.
proposals.novelty: While distillation and progressive network scaling exist, the explicit use of a distilled *policy* as a behavioral prior to guide and accelerate the training of a *larger* model is a non-obvious inversion of the standard compression flow. The integration of this idea with asynchronous, distributed training frameworks like Local-SGD and DiPaCo for scalable expansion is a novel technical synthesis.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-D: Distributed Path Decomposition via Anti-Learning for Task-Specialized Models
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task and transfer learning paradigms aim to share representations, but this can lead to negative interference and catastrophic forgetting when tasks conflict. As models like Gemini scale in capability and are deployed across diverse, specialized tasks, there is a need for efficient methods to actively *prevent* harmful parameter sharing, ensuring stable performance and enabling the extraction of clean, task-specific sub-networks from a base model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying an anti-learning objective—actively decorrelating the weight updates for distinct tasks during distributed training—can create a more modular parameter space within a single model, from which specialized, non-interfering sub-networks can be efficiently extracted.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo framework for distributed path composition by integrating a novel anti-learning loss. During asynchronous local-SGD phases on different task batches, we will compute a gradient similarity penalty between local updates and a running average of updates from other tasks, pushing their directions apart. This will be combined with a sparsity-inducing regularizer on the final composition step to encourage a factorized parameter space. The resulting model will be evaluated on its ability to yield task-specialized 'expert' sub-networks via simple masking, assessed on multi-task benchmarks and transfer scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel distributed training algorithm for learning modular, anti-correlated representations within a single large model. It will provide a formal framework and empirical evidence for the controlled use of negative transfer, advancing techniques for model specialization and interference reduction. The outcome will be a method to efficiently produce a family of stable, task-specialized models from a unified training process.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on negative transfer seeks to avoid it, we propose to actively induce and harness it as a training signal for decomposition. This inverts the standard goal of transfer learning and combines it with the distributed, compositional training of DiPaCo to achieve a new form of model modularity.

proposals.title: Lifelong Robotic Skill Acquisition via Distributed Elastic Weight Consolidation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic learning systems are typically trained on a single, static dataset or task, failing to adapt to a continuous stream of new sensorimotor experiences. For robots to be truly useful in dynamic, real-world environments, they must learn sequentially without catastrophically forgetting previous skills. While methods like Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) show promise in simulation, scaling them to a physical robot learning many tasks over a long lifetime remains an unsolved challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed, asynchronous training framework, inspired by recent advances in large-scale language model training, can enable the effective application of EWC for lifelong learning on a physical robot, allowing it to accumulate a diverse repertoire of skills without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will implement a novel Distributed EWC (DiEWC) system on a physical robot platform. The core learner will be a large multimodal model (e.g., a Gemini-inspired architecture) processing proprioception, vision, and force data. Inspired by the DiPaCo and Asynchronous Local-SGD frameworks, we will deploy a distributed network of 'skill anchors'—each a local model specializing in a previously learned task. The main model will be trained on new tasks via asynchronous local updates, with EWC penalties computed and enforced across the distributed network to protect the consolidated parameters of past skills, enabling efficient, non-blocking continual learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first demonstration of scalable, distributed continual learning on a physical robot over an extended operational period. It will provide a concrete architecture and open-source framework for lifelong robotic learning, bridging the gap between theoretical continual learning algorithms and practical, long-term embodied AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synthesis of distributed, asynchronous training paradigms from large-scale ML with neuro-inspired continual learning algorithms (EWC) for embodied agents, moving beyond proof-of-concept simulations to a sustained, physical robot deployment.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning as Path Optimization: A Distributed, Asynchronous Framework for Task Sequencing in Multitask Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale multitask training, as seen in models like Gemini, often treats task order as fixed or random, potentially missing significant gains in sample efficiency and final performance. The impact of task sequence on knowledge transfer remains poorly understood, especially in distributed, long-context training regimes. This work addresses a core gap in curriculum learning for modern, agentic multimodal models.
proposals.hypothesis: Optimal task sequences for transfer learning can be discovered through a learned, compositional path over a task manifold, and training with this curriculum will yield superior performance and faster convergence compared to fixed or random ordering.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DiPaCo distributed training framework to treat task sequences as paths to be optimized. Using a lightweight meta-controller, we will asynchronously evaluate candidate curricula (task orders) across local-SGD workers, inspired by the efficiency of Asynchronous Local-SGD. The controller will learn to compose tasks by maximizing a transfer utility metric, dynamically scheduling tasks to each worker to minimize negative interference and maximize forward transfer, akin to efficient pathfinding in a graph of tasks.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a scalable, data-driven algorithm for automatic curriculum design in multitask learning. It will yield new empirical insights into the principles governing task transfer in large models. The resulting framework will be a practical tool for improving the training efficiency of next-generation multimodal and agentic systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior curriculum learning, which is often heuristic, we frame it as a distributed path composition problem, integrating asynchronous evaluation with a learned meta-controller. This directly connects the scalability of DiPaCo with the task-sequencing question, a novel synthesis of the researcher's recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: Thinking-in-Time: Training Small, Fast Models with Iterative, Asynchronous Reasoning Loops
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for high-reasoning models (e.g., Gemini, GPT-4) relies on massive scale, making them slow and expensive to run. For real-time, on-device, or high-throughput agentic applications, this is prohibitive. We need a new paradigm that decouples reasoning depth from model size and inference latency.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, fast base model can achieve deep reasoning capabilities by learning to execute structured, iterative 'thinking' loops over time, where each step is a lightweight, specialized computation that refines an internal state, mimicking the deliberation of larger models.
proposals.method: We will design a compact transformer-based core (e.g., <1B params) augmented with a persistent, evolving 'reasoning state' memory. Inspired by Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo's compositional training, we will train this model using a curriculum of reasoning tasks (e.g., chain-of-thought QA, planning). The key is an asynchronous, multi-timescale training regime where the model learns to perform many fast, local 'thinking' updates (like Flash attention steps) to its internal state before producing a final output, effectively distributing reasoning over time instead of parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of models that are both small and capable of deep, deliberate reasoning, enabling efficient deployment. It will provide a novel training framework for instilling iterative computation in neural networks. The resulting models and methods will directly advance the development of next-generation, efficient agentic systems.
proposals.novelty: While large models use scale for reasoning and prior small models are limited, we explicitly train a small model to perform *temporally extended* reasoning via learned internal loops, combining insights from asynchronous optimization, agentic loops, and efficient architectures in a new way.

proposals.title: Dense Reward Composition via Distributed Path Conditioning for Complex Task Learning
proposals.motivation: Sparse rewards remain a fundamental bottleneck in reinforcement learning (RL) and agent training, leading to high sample inefficiency and exploration challenges, especially in long-horizon or complex reasoning tasks. This is acutely relevant for training large-scale models like Gemini on open-ended agentic capabilities, where defining a single terminal reward is insufficient. Pre-2026, methods like reward shaping are often heuristic and lack scalability, while imitation learning can suffer from distributional shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scalable, distributed method for learning dense, temporally-extended reward functions from a combination of sparse success signals and sub-task demonstrations can dramatically improve the sample efficiency and final performance of RL agents on complex, long-context tasks.
proposals.method: We propose to extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to learn a dense reward composer. Multiple expert workers, each trained via Asynchronous Local-SGD on distinct sub-tasks or demonstration datasets, will produce local value or advantage estimates. A central composer model will learn to dynamically weight and combine these local signals into a dense, composite reward function, conditioned on the agent's current state and trajectory. This reward will then be used to train a primary policy model, potentially leveraging Flash Attention for efficient long-context reasoning during both reward computation and policy learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable algorithm for automatic dense reward generation that reduces reliance on manual reward engineering. It will demonstrate significant improvements in sample efficiency and success rate on a suite of complex, long-horizon benchmarks, providing a path toward more autonomous and efficient training of next-generation agentic models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the distributed, compositional training paradigm of DiPaCo—previously used for model parameters—to the problem of reward function synthesis, creating a learned, adaptive alternative to fixed reward shaping. This integrates demonstration-based sub-task learning with sparse global reward in a unified, scalable system.

proposals.title: Progressive Nets for Complex Multi-Stage Manipulation: A Path Composition Approach
proposals.motivation: Progressive Networks (Rusu et al., 2016) demonstrated a powerful paradigm for transfer and lifelong learning by laterally connecting columns of networks, preventing catastrophic forgetting. However, their application has been largely confined to relatively simple, low-dimensional control tasks. Scaling this framework to complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks with high-dimensional sensory inputs (e.g., vision) remains an open challenge, crucial for building versatile, continually learning robotic agents.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating the Progressive Networks framework with modern large-scale training paradigms like DiPaCo and efficient asynchronous local training methods, we can construct scalable, multi-column architectures capable of learning and composing skills for complex, multi-stage manipulation tasks without forgetting.
proposals.method: We will extend the Progressive Networks architecture using a DiPaCo-inspired distributed path composition strategy, where each task or skill sub-policy is trained as a local column. We will employ asynchronous Local-SGD for efficient parallel training of columns. Lateral connections will be implemented via learned, sparsely gated adapters (inspired by recent Mixture-of-Experts work) to enable flexible knowledge transfer. The framework will be evaluated on a suite of complex, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks in simulated and real-world robotic settings, building upon tasks from the Gemini project's multimodal reasoning domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, practical implementation of Progressive Networks for high-dimensional, complex tasks, demonstrating significant improvements in sample efficiency and sequential task performance over fine-tuning and other multi-task baselines. It will provide a concrete architecture and training methodology for lifelong learning in robotics, bridging a gap between a seminal idea and modern large-scale ML systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the classical Progressive Networks idea with contemporary distributed training (DiPaCo) and asynchronous optimization methods to overcome the original framework's scalability limitations, applying it to a new domain of complex manipulation which demands both perceptual and motor composition.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-Sim: Distributed Path Composition for Robust Transfer Across Visuo-Dynamic Simulation Gaps
proposals.motivation: Training agents in simulation is efficient, but transferring learned policies to new environments suffers from visual and dynamic discrepancies, a core challenge for real-world robotics and embodied AI. Current domain adaptation methods often struggle with large, multi-faceted shifts in rendering style, physics, and object properties simultaneously. This limits the utility of large-scale, heterogeneous simulation datasets for building generalizable agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed, modular training framework, inspired by DiPaCo, can learn a composition of robust, environment-agnostic sub-policies that transfer more effectively across large visual and dynamic gaps than monolithic models or standard fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Distributed Path Composition (DiPaCo) framework to the multi-environment RL setting. Each 'expert' pathway will be trained asynchronously (inspired by Local-SGD) on a distinct source simulation (e.g., MuJoCo, PyBullet, different DR domains). A shared sparse router, conditioned on task and a learned embedding of environment characteristics, will compose these experts. We will leverage pre-trained visual encoders (e.g., from Gemini's multimodal lineage) for perception, freezing them initially to focus on dynamics transfer. Training will use a mixture of task-specific and domain-invariant alignment losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable method for learning policies robust to significant simulation-to-simulation gaps, advancing the field of transfer for embodied AI. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of distributed, compositional architectures over monolithic models for cross-domain generalization in continuous control tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the DiPaCo paradigm—designed for scaling language models—to the distinct problem of visuo-dynamic transfer in RL, and explicitly training for compositionality across asynchronous, heterogeneous simulation sources rather than a single source-target pair.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-Vision: Distributed Composition of Low-Level Features for Efficient Multi-Task Visual Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale models are often trained from scratch for new tasks, ignoring the potential of reusing learned, transferable low-level features. This is computationally wasteful and limits rapid adaptation. Progressive networks demonstrated the value of lateral connections for transfer, but their scaling to many tasks and distributed training remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed, composable architecture based on the principles of progressive networks and DiPaCo will enable the efficient, asynchronous reuse of low-level visual features across a large set of tasks, leading to superior data efficiency and faster convergence compared to independent or joint training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework for vision. A set of foundational 'expert' modules, trained asynchronously on diverse low-level visual tasks (e.g., edge detection, texture recognition), will be frozen. New task-specific 'head' networks will learn to compose these experts via learned, sparse gating mechanisms, inspired by mixture-of-experts and progressive network lateral connections. Training will leverage asynchronous local-SGD for the task heads, communicating only gating weight updates, while the foundational experts remain static.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, distributed method for lifelong visual feature reuse, reducing compute for new tasks. It will yield a benchmark for compositional visual transfer and insights into which low-level features are universally useful. The resulting system will demonstrate efficient few-shot adaptation by composing existing features.
proposals.novelty: While progressive networks are sequential and DiPaCo is for language, this proposal uniquely combines their principles for distributed, multi-task visual learning. The focus on asynchronous, local training of composable heads over a fixed, pre-trained low-level feature library is a novel systems-and-learning hybrid approach.

proposals.title: Multi-Source Progressive Networks: Scaling Knowledge Transfer with Distributed Path Composition
proposals.motivation: Progressive Networks (PNs) demonstrated strong transfer learning by freezing source columns and adding lateral connections to a new column, but scaling to many source tasks remains a challenge. As models like Gemini push frontiers in multimodality and reasoning, efficiently composing knowledge from diverse, pre-trained expert models (e.g., for vision, language, robotics) is crucial for building generalist agents without catastrophic forgetting or prohibitive compute costs.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed, multi-source Progressive Network architecture, leveraging asynchronous training and path composition principles, can efficiently integrate knowledge from a large set of diverse source tasks, leading to faster convergence and superior performance on novel target tasks compared to single-source transfer or monolithic multi-task training.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to implement a scalable multi-source PN. Each source task will be represented by a pre-trained, frozen expert column. A new target column will be trained asynchronously (inspired by Asynchronous Local-SGD) using lateral connections to a dynamically sampled subset of relevant source columns per batch, determined by task similarity. The training will employ a mixture-of-experts style gating mechanism to compose paths from the active source columns into the target network, optimizing for parameter efficiency and transfer performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable blueprint for lifelong learning and rapid adaptation of large models by demonstrating how to effectively harness a library of pre-trained experts. It will yield empirical results on the benefits of multi-source transfer at scale and a new distributed training paradigm for modular, composable neural architectures.
proposals.novelty: While PNs and expert mixtures exist, combining them within a distributed, asynchronously trained path composition framework to handle *many* source tasks is novel. This directly addresses the scalability limitations of original PNs and aligns with the trend towards modular, composable models like DiPaCo and the multi-modal capabilities of systems like Gemini.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-ADA: Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Simulation-to-Real Transfer in Distributed Path Composition
proposals.motivation: Training large-scale models like Gemini on real-world data is often costly, risky, or data-limited. Simulation offers a scalable, safe alternative for generating training data, but the inevitable domain shift between simulation and reality degrades model performance. Efficiently aligning these domains is a critical bottleneck for applying large models in robotics, autonomous systems, and other embodied AI tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating adversarial domain adaptation directly into the asynchronous, distributed training framework of DiPaCo will enable more efficient and robust alignment of simulation and real-world feature spaces, leading to superior transfer performance compared to post-hoc adaptation methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework by introducing an adversarial domain discriminator module that operates on the features of selected model pathways. Inspired by adversarial methods like DANN, this module will be trained to distinguish between simulation and real features, while the feature extractors are trained to fool it, promoting domain-invariant representations. The adversarial loss will be integrated asynchronously alongside local-SGD updates, allowing for scalable, communication-efficient adaptation across the distributed training system.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, scalable method for simulation-to-real transfer within a state-of-the-art distributed training paradigm. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of integrating adversarial alignment into asynchronous, modular training, advancing the practical application of large models in real-world settings where simulation is a key data source.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial domain adaptation is well-studied, its integration into the asynchronous, pathway-based DiPaCo framework is novel. This moves beyond applying adaptation to a monolithic model, instead enabling dynamic, distributed alignment of specific model components during large-scale training.

proposals.title: Meta-Adapt: Fast Adaptation of Progressive Neural Networks via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Large-scale models like Gemini excel at broad tasks but struggle to adapt efficiently to new, specific real-world conditions (e.g., novel sensor data, domain-specific jargon) without costly full fine-tuning. Progressive networks and distributed training methods like DiPaCo offer a path for continual learning, but their adaptation speed for new conditions remains a bottleneck. Meta-learning, which learns a model initialization for fast adaptation, provides a promising but underexplored solution for accelerating this process within modern large-scale training paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML) into the training of progressive or modular architectures (like DiPaCo) will yield a base model that can rapidly adapt to new tasks or domains with significantly fewer gradient steps and less data than standard transfer learning, without compromising the stability of asynchronous or local training schemes.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DiPaCo distributed path composition framework, treating each expert module as a potential adaptation point. During a meta-training phase, we will simulate adaptation scenarios by sampling batches from a distribution of related tasks (e.g., different languages, specialized QA formats). We will apply a first-order MAML-like algorithm to learn initial parameters for the progressive network that are sensitive to task-specific loss gradients, enabling fast adaptation via a few steps of local SGD on a new condition. The training will leverage asynchronous local-SGD techniques to maintain scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable method for meta-learning within large, modular neural networks, demonstrating quantifiable gains in few-shot adaptation speed for large language models. It will provide empirical evidence on how meta-initializations interact with distributed, progressive training paradigms, offering a path toward more agile and efficient post-deployment model personalization.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and progressive networks are established separately, their integration for accelerating the adaptation of billion-parameter models trained with advanced distributed methods (DiPaCo, async local-SGD) is novel. The key innovation is formulating the meta-learning objective over a composed, modular architecture rather than a monolithic model, enabling targeted and efficient adaptation.

proposals.title: Sim2Real Curriculum Learning: Bridging the Reality Gap with Task-Conditioned Progressive Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Training agents in simulation is efficient but suffers from the 'reality gap'—a performance drop when deployed in the real world due to domain mismatch. Pre-2026, large-scale models like Gemini demonstrate strong reasoning and multimodal capabilities, but efficiently transferring these skills from synthetic to physical domains remains a costly and unsolved bottleneck for embodied AI and robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring a curriculum of tasks, ordered by increasing domain complexity and realism, will enable more sample-efficient and robust transfer from simulation to reality than direct fine-tuning or naive domain randomization.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large, multitask language-vision-action model (e.g., a Gemini-inspired architecture) pre-trained on diverse simulated tasks. Using a DiPaCo-like distributed training framework, we will train a lightweight, task-conditioned adapter module. The curriculum will be defined by a 'realism score' blending visual texture complexity, physical dynamics fidelity, and task difficulty, generated via a learned function. Training will proceed asynchronously (inspired by Local-SGD) across a mixture of simulation and real-world data streams, gradually increasing the proportion and realism of real data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable framework for Sim2Real transfer, reducing real-world data requirements. It will yield a novel method for automatic curriculum generation based on domain characteristics and demonstrate its efficacy on benchmark robotic manipulation and navigation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of automatic, multi-faceted curriculum generation with the efficient, distributed training of large pre-trained models, moving beyond hand-designed domain randomization or fixed, linear curricula.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-Robotics: Distributed Path Composition for Multi-Robot Progressive Learning
proposals.motivation: Training robust, generalizable robot control policies requires vast and diverse real-world data, which is a severe bottleneck due to the time and cost of single-robot data collection. Distributed learning methods like DiPaCo and Local-SGD have shown promise for scaling model training across compute clusters, but their application to distributed, asynchronous data collection across heterogeneous physical robots remains unexplored. This work aims to bridge this gap, enabling efficient, parallelized learning directly from the physical world.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the Distributed Path Composition (DiPaCo) framework to a fleet of heterogeneous robots, we can achieve scalable, sample-efficient progressive network training where each robot contributes specialized, locally learned skills that are asynchronously composed into a globally capable policy.
proposals.method: We will deploy a fleet of robots with varying morphologies and sensors, each running an independent local training loop using an adapted Asynchronous Local-SGD algorithm to update a shared base model. Inspired by DiPaCo, each robot will act as a distinct 'expert path', with its local updates representing a specialized skill trajectory. A central coordinator will periodically compose these paths via a learned gating function, integrating diverse physical experiences into a unified policy. The system will use Flash Attention for efficient on-robot inference and gradient computation within long context windows of sensorimotor data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel distributed learning system that scales policy training with the number of physical robots, dramatically accelerating real-world data acquisition. It will provide empirical benchmarks for multi-robot distributed learning and a new open-source framework, DiPaCo-Robotics, for the community. We will demonstrate significant improvements in sample efficiency and final task performance on complex, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While DiPaCo composes paths across computational nodes, this is the first work to apply the paradigm to distributed, asynchronous learning across *physical robotic agents*, where each path embodies not just a data partition but a unique embodiment and environmental interaction. It uniquely combines the robot-as-expert concept with the scalable synchronization mechanics of pre-2026 distributed training methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Transfer: Learning a Generative Simulation Prior from Real-World Policy Data
proposals.motivation: Sim-to-real transfer is a cornerstone of efficient robot learning, but it assumes a high-fidelity simulator is available and correctly parameterized. In many real-world settings, such as complex manipulation or human interaction, building an accurate simulator is prohibitively difficult. This work investigates the inverse problem: using policies trained directly in the real world to infer or improve a simulation model, thereby closing the loop and enabling more efficient future training cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the behavioral data from a policy trained to mastery in the real world contains sufficient information to train a generative model of a simplified simulation environment, and that policies fine-tuned in this inferred simulation will retain strong real-world performance more efficiently than training from scratch.
proposals.method: We will leverage a two-stage approach. First, we will collect extensive state-action trajectories from a high-performing real-world policy (e.g., a Gemini-based agent). Second, we will train a conditional diffusion model or a world model (e.g., a masked autoencoder) to generate plausible next-state transitions given the current state and action, effectively learning a 'local' simulation dynamics prior. This model will be trained using asynchronous local-SGD principles across multiple real-world deployment logs to handle non-stationary data. Finally, we will use this learned simulation for rapid policy iteration via DiPaCo-style distributed path composition, transferring the refined policy back to the real world for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical algorithms for real-to-simulation transfer, a largely underexplored direction. It will demonstrate how to distill real-world experience into a reusable, trainable simulation prior, reducing dependency on hand-crafted simulators. The outcome is a method for bootstrapping and continuously improving simulation environments directly from deployment data.
proposals.novelty: While inverse reinforcement learning and system identification exist, our approach is novel in using policy mastery data (not just demonstrations) to learn a generative, agent-centric simulation model specifically for efficient policy refinement, tightly integrating large-scale agent training (Gemini) with distributed, asynchronous model learning techniques from the researcher's recent work.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-T: Distributed Path Composition for Long-Horizon Task Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current large language and multimodal models, such as Gemini, excel at short-horizon reasoning but struggle with tasks requiring long sequences of interdependent actions, like complex software engineering or multi-step scientific reasoning. Scaling to such long-horizon, sequential tasks is a critical frontier for achieving more general and capable AI agents. Pre-2026 methods often face prohibitive computational and memory costs when training on or transferring to these extended sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modular, distributed training framework, inspired by DiPaCo, can efficiently scale to long-horizon tasks by composing and reusing specialized sub-task modules learned asynchronously, thereby overcoming the limitations of monolithic end-to-end training.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to sequential domains. The core method involves training a population of specialized, reusable 'skill modules' asynchronously on diverse, shorter sub-sequences of long-horizon tasks using Local-SGD. A central lightweight router network, trained with a novel temporal credit assignment mechanism, will learn to dynamically compose these modules into a coherent sequence of actions for a target long-horizon task, leveraging Flash Attention for efficient long-context reasoning during composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, modular paradigm for training and transferring knowledge to long-horizon sequential tasks, significantly reducing compute requirements compared to monolithic models. It will also deliver a concrete framework, DiPaCo-T, and benchmarks demonstrating efficient learning of complex, multi-step procedural tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the modular, distributed training philosophy of DiPaCo with the challenges of long-horizon sequential decision-making, moving beyond static mixture-of-experts to dynamic, temporally-aware composition of asynchronous modules for action sequences.

proposals.title: Task-Specific Sparse Subnetworks via Distributed Path Composition and Asynchronous Local Training
proposals.motivation: Continual learning and multi-task adaptation often rely on adding entire new network columns or modules, leading to linear parameter growth and inefficient inference. This is unsustainable for scaling to thousands of tasks, especially in large language models where full fine-tuning is prohibitively expensive. A more parameter-efficient, composable, and inference-friendly paradigm is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that each new task can be mastered by learning a sparse, task-specific subnetwork within a shared, frozen foundation model, and that these subnetworks can be composed additively without interference, inverting the need for separate columns.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to learn sparse, task-specific 'paths' (subsets of parameters) within a model like Gemini. Each path will be trained asynchronously using a Local-SGD variant for efficiency. At inference, the frozen base model's activations will be additively modified only by the active, sparse weights of the composed task-specific paths, enabling multi-task capability without expanding the active parameter count.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a method for adding new tasks with sub-linear parameter growth and constant inference cost. It will provide a theoretical and empirical framework for sparse, additive task composition, advancing beyond modular or adapter-based approaches. The result is a step towards a single, scalable model that can host an ever-growing library of tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining the sparse path discovery of DiPaCo with the asynchronous training efficiency of Local-SGD to achieve additive, non-interfering task composition within a single model, a departure from the dominant column-based or sequential fine-tuning paradigms.

proposals.title: Task-Agnostic Meta-Training for Robust Multi-Task and Agentic Learning
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task and agentic training paradigms, such as those explored in Gemini 2.5, often rely on explicit task identifiers or prompts to condition model behavior, creating a brittle assumption that may not hold in open-world deployment. This requirement limits the model's ability to generalize to novel, unseen tasks and to operate robustly in environments where task boundaries are ambiguous. Relaxing this constraint is a critical step toward more autonomous and adaptable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by training a model to infer task identity implicitly from context and learned internal representations, we can achieve performance parity with task-conditioned models while significantly improving robustness and generalization to task variants and compositions.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-training framework based on a distributed, asynchronous Local-SGD setup (inspired by Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo) to efficiently train a large-scale model on a massive, diverse mixture of tasks without explicit task IDs. The core innovation is a 'task-inference router' module that uses a sparse, mixture-of-experts architecture to dynamically compose pathways (leveraging DiPaCo principles) based on the input context alone. Training will employ a combination of next-token prediction and an auxiliary contrastive loss that encourages the router to cluster latent representations by implicit task semantics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable method for training task-agnostic foundation models, reducing reliance on engineered prompts and improving zero-shot generalization. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating implicit task understanding in multi-task and agentic settings, pushing the frontier of autonomous capability as outlined in the Gemini research agenda.
proposals.novelty: While mixture-of-experts and multi-task learning are established, combining them with an explicit objective to eliminate task IDs during training—using a distributed, path-composition framework for scalability—is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond simply removing the ID to actively teaching the model to infer and specialize dynamically.

proposals.title: The Transfer Tax: Quantifying Negative Lateral Transfer in Modular and Continual Learning Systems
proposals.motivation: Modular and continual learning systems, such as those based on distributed path composition (DiPaCo) or progressive neural networks, rely heavily on lateral transfer from prior learned components (columns) to accelerate learning of new tasks. The prevailing assumption is that such transfer is universally beneficial. However, in complex, non-stationary environments, negative transfer—where prior knowledge hinders new learning—is a known but poorly understood risk, potentially undermining the scalability and robustness of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lateral transfer becomes detrimental when the statistical structure of a new task is misaligned with the inductive biases encoded in a prior column, and that this negative effect can be predicted and mitigated by analyzing gradient alignment and activation sparsity during early training.
