proposals.method: We will extend the Asynchronous Local-SGD framework to simulate a multi-column learning system, where each column is trained on a sequence of related tasks (e.g., from the Gemini multimodal benchmark). We will instrument lateral connections to monitor transfer via gradient cosine similarity and activation overlap. Controlled experiments will vary task similarity and column specialization. A core technical innovation will be a 'transfer gate' mechanism, inspired by adaptive methods like FlashAttention's efficiency, that dynamically modulates lateral transfer based on real-time performance metrics on the new task's local validation loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of negative lateral transfer in modular deep learning, yielding quantitative metrics for 'transfer interference.' It will also propose and validate a lightweight, adaptive gating mechanism to make lateral transfer selectively beneficial, directly improving the sample efficiency and final performance of continual and distributed learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While negative transfer is studied in classical multi-task and domain adaptation settings, its analysis within the specific context of asynchronous, modular architectures like DiPaCo and progressive networks is novel. The proposed dynamic gating approach moves beyond static architectures or fixed penalties, offering an online, learning-driven solution.

proposals.title: ProgressiveNet: Lifelong Learning for Complex Supervised Tasks via Lateral Connections and Knowledge Transfer
proposals.motivation: Progressive Networks, introduced for reinforcement learning, elegantly prevent catastrophic forgetting by instantiating new columns of parameters for new tasks while leveraging prior knowledge via lateral connections. However, their application to large-scale, complex supervised domains like ImageNet-scale classification or sequence-to-sequence NLP tasks remains underexplored. This research addresses the critical need for lifelong learning systems that can accumulate and reuse knowledge across a long sequence of diverse supervised tasks without performance degradation on earlier ones, a key challenge in pre-2026 machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Progressive Network architecture, enhanced with modern parameter-efficient lateral connections (e.g., adapters, cross-attention) and asynchronous training methods, can be scaled effectively to complex supervised domains, achieving superior lifelong learning performance compared to fine-tuning, replay-based, or regularization-based continual learning baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core Progressive Network architecture for supervised learning. For each new task, a new column (e.g., a ViT or Transformer encoder) is instantiated and trained. Crucially, lateral connections from all previous columns to the new one will be implemented using parameter-efficient modules like adapters or lightweight cross-attention layers, inspired by recent transfer learning work. We will investigate training strategies from the researcher's recent work, such as Asynchronous Local-SGD, to manage the computational and memory overhead of multiple columns. Evaluation will be conducted on sequences of image classification (e.g., split CIFAR-100, sequential ImageNet subsets) and NLP tasks (e.g., GLUE benchmark tasks in sequence).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, large-scale demonstration of Progressive Networks in complex supervised settings, establishing a new strong baseline for lifelong learning. It will yield insights into scalable lateral connection designs and contribute a novel, practical framework for continual learning that inherently avoids forgetting, directly applicable to scenarios requiring sequential model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling and re-engineering the Progressive Network—a concept primarily validated in RL—for the distinct challenges of high-dimensional supervised learning, combining it with contemporary parameter-efficient transfer techniques and asynchronous distributed training to make it computationally feasible.

proposals.title: Progressive Networks at Extreme Scale: Lifelong Learning Across Hundreds of Sequential Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current lifelong learning methods are tested on short sequences of 5-20 tasks, failing to capture the true scale and complexity of continual learning in real-world systems. This gap limits our understanding of catastrophic forgetting and forward transfer over long horizons. Scaling to hundreds of tasks is crucial for developing models that can accumulate knowledge over extended operational lifetimes, a core challenge for generalist agents like Gemini.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Progressive Networks architecture, enhanced with asynchronous local training and dynamic path composition, can maintain stable performance and positive forward transfer across sequences of hundreds of diverse tasks, where standard fine-tuning and replay-based methods will catastrophically fail.
proposals.method: We will extend the Progressive Networks framework, where a new column of parameters is added per task, preventing forgetting. To manage the parameter explosion, we will integrate techniques from DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) for efficient, sparse activation of task-specific pathways. Training will leverage asynchronous Local-SGD to parallelize learning across tasks and hardware, while a shared flash-attention-based backbone will enable efficient cross-task reasoning and knowledge extraction from long-context data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical benchmark for lifelong learning at an unprecedented scale of hundreds of tasks. It will yield practical insights into the scalability limits of parameter-isolation methods and produce a novel, efficient architecture for continual learning that bridges advances in distributed training and large-scale modeling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the massive scaling of the sequential task benchmark and the synthesis of Progressive Networks with modern large-scale distributed training paradigms (DiPaCo, Async Local-SGD) to solve the associated systems and efficiency challenges, which has not been explored.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Progressive Networks: Dynamic Task Sequencing for Efficient Multi-Task Transfer
proposals.motivation: Progressive networks and related architectures like Pathways offer a powerful paradigm for multi-task learning and continual adaptation by adding new columns of parameters for new tasks. However, the sequence in which tasks are introduced is typically arbitrary, which may lead to suboptimal knowledge transfer and slower convergence. Curriculum learning, which strategically orders training data or tasks by difficulty, has shown benefits in single-task settings but remains underexplored for structuring the training of modular, multi-task neural systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying curriculum learning principles to order the sequence of tasks during the training of a progressive network will improve final performance, accelerate training convergence, and enhance positive forward transfer compared to random or heuristic task ordering.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to create a progressive network where new task-specific columns are added over time. We will design a curriculum scheduler that dynamically sequences tasks based on pre-computed metrics of task similarity and difficulty, informed by representations from a base model (e.g., a Gemini variant). The training will leverage asynchronous local-SGD for efficient distributed training across the evolving architecture. The core experiment will compare curriculum-based ordering against baselines (random, fixed heuristic) on a benchmark suite of related NLP and vision tasks, measuring final accuracy, time to convergence, and transfer effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for task sequencing in progressive learning systems, potentially leading to more sample-efficient and performant multi-task models. It will yield empirical results and a theoretical framework for understanding how task curricula influence modular neural network learning dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is well-studied for data within a single task, its application to orchestrate the macroscopic sequence of distinct tasks in a parameter-expanding progressive network is novel. This combines insights from modular architectures, distributed training, and curriculum learning in a unique way not explored in prior work.

proposals.title: PathComposition-TL: Enhancing Forward Transfer in Sequential Multi-Task Learning via Distributed Path Composition
proposals.motivation: Training large models on diverse, sequential tasks often suffers from catastrophic forgetting and inefficient knowledge reuse. While multi-task learning and transfer learning are established, methods for ensuring consistent positive forward transfer—where learning one task systematically improves performance on future related tasks—remain ad-hoc. This is critical for developing agents that can continuously learn and adapt over time without extensive retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the learning trajectory across tasks using a distributed, composable pathway architecture will yield stronger and more reliable positive forward transfer compared to standard sequential fine-tuning or monolithic multi-task training.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to train a set of expert pathways on a curriculum of related sequential tasks (e.g., a series of reasoning or multimodal tasks). We will adapt techniques from asynchronous Local-SGD to coordinate the training of these distributed pathways over time, ensuring stable optimization. A central router, inspired by mixture-of-experts and transfer learning principles, will be learned to compose relevant prior pathways when encountering a new task, actively promoting forward transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic framework for controlled knowledge transfer in sequential learning settings. It will yield empirical benchmarks demonstrating superior forward transfer on curated task sequences compared to strong baselines. The resulting methodology will offer a scalable alternative to full model retraining for continual learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the distributed, composable DiPaCo architecture with a time-aware, curriculum-driven training regimen specifically designed to maximize forward transfer, moving beyond static multi-task training or simple fine-tuning chains.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Transfer via Task-Driven Source Domain Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Modern representation learning for transfer relies heavily on vast amounts of unlabeled source domain data (e.g., ImageNet, web-scale corpora), which is expensive, privacy-sensitive, and often unavailable for specialized domains. This creates a significant bottleneck for applying large-scale pre-training paradigms to new, data-scarce tasks. Challenging this reliance could democratize high-performance model development.
proposals.hypothesis: Effective, transferable representations can be learned from a minimal or synthetic source domain, generated or guided by the structure of the target tasks, rather than from massive, generic unsupervised data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we use a lightweight, rapidly trainable generator (e.g., a diffusion model or a small GAN) to synthesize source domain data, where the generation process is conditioned not on real data statistics but on a learned prior informed by the target task distribution via meta-learning. Second, we apply a distributed, compositionally trained model (extending DiPaCo principles) where multiple specialized 'local' experts are trained asynchronously (inspired by Asynchronous Local-SGD) on different synthetic data regimes and their knowledge is composed for robust transfer to diverse downstream tasks, minimizing reliance on any single real data source.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a novel methodology for data-efficient pre-training, reducing dependency on large-scale data collection. It would offer theoretical and empirical insights into the essential characteristics of source data needed for transfer. The resulting framework would be a step towards more sustainable and accessible representation learning.
proposals.novelty: Instead of filtering or curating existing unsupervised data, we synthesize a task-informed source domain from scratch. The approach uniquely combines generative modeling for data creation with distributed, compositional training architectures for robust transfer learning.

proposals.title: Interleaved Pathways: Co-optimizing Representations and Policies for Efficient Agent Learning
proposals.motivation: Current agent training pipelines, especially in large multimodal models like Gemini, often rely on a rigid 'see then act' paradigm: a foundational model learns static representations, followed by a separate, often expensive, policy fine-tuning stage. This sequential approach can lead to suboptimal, task-specific representations and inefficient use of compute. As agents tackle increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks, a more integrated learning process is needed to improve sample efficiency and final performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interleaving and co-optimizing representation learning with policy learning objectives within a single, unified training framework will yield more adaptable and performant agents than strictly sequential training, by allowing the representations to be dynamically shaped by downstream decision-making needs.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Distributed Path Composition (DiPaCo) framework and Asynchronous Local-SGD to design a multi-pathway architecture. One pathway focuses on general representation learning (the 'see' component), while parallel, interleaved pathways are dedicated to specific policy or skill learning (the 'act' component). We will employ a novel, temporally-aware gating mechanism—inspired by Mixture-of-Experts and Flash attention—to dynamically route and blend gradients between these pathways during training, enabling continuous, bidirectional information flow between representation and policy modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural and algorithmic framework for joint representation-policy optimization. It will demonstrate measurable gains in sample efficiency and final task performance on a suite of long-context, multimodal benchmarks derived from the Gemini lineage. The resulting method will offer a scalable alternative to the traditional pre-train then fine-tune paradigm for agent development.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning and multi-task training are well-established, the explicit, architectural interleaving of representation and policy gradients within a single distributed training run, using dynamic routing informed by temporal task progress, is a novel departure from the strictly sequential paradigm.

proposals.title: Zero-Shot Transfer of Disentangled Representations for Real-World Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in robotics is enabling agents to generalize across diverse environments and tasks without costly retraining. Pre-2026, DARLA (DisentAngled Representation Learning Agent) demonstrated impressive zero-shot transfer in simulated visual domains by learning domain-invariant representations. However, its efficacy on physical robots, which face complex, noisy sensory data and embodiment constraints, remains untested and is a critical next step for sample-efficient real-world learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the disentangled, domain-invariant visual representations learned by DARLA in simulation will enable zero-shot generalization to real-world robotic manipulation tasks, significantly outperforming standard end-to-end learned policies when faced with novel visual conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DARLA framework, which uses β-VAE for unsupervised state-space factorization, for robotic control. A policy network will be trained in simulation using the disentangled representations. We will then deploy this policy in a zero-shot manner on a physical robot arm (e.g., Franka Emika Panda) equipped with a standard RGB camera. The primary testbed will be object grasping and placement under varying lighting, backgrounds, and object textures not seen in simulation. We will benchmark against a standard RL-from-pixels baseline (e.g., SAC) and an agent trained with domain randomization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical validation of DARLA's disentangled representation transfer on a physical robotic system. It will yield concrete performance benchmarks for zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and identify failure modes specific to embodiment and actuator dynamics. The findings will inform future work on combining representation learning with robotic control architectures.
proposals.novelty: While domain randomization and adaptation are common, directly testing DARLA's *unsupervised* disentanglement for zero-shot real-world robotics is novel. The experiment probes whether the high-level visual factors learned in simulation are truly invariant to the reality gap, a non-obvious claim given the complexity of real sensorimotor loops.

proposals.title: Domain-Adversarial Feature Learning for Robust and Transferable Reinforcement Learning Agents
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) is the brittleness of agents to distributional shifts in state and dynamics, which severely limits real-world deployment and sample-efficient transfer across tasks. Domain Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN), proven effective in computer vision for learning domain-invariant features, offer a principled framework to address this. This project aims to adapt and rigorously evaluate DANN's principles to learn robust, domain-invariant representations within RL, directly tackling the generalization gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a domain-adversarial objective into an RL agent's representation learning pipeline will force the extraction of domain-invariant features, leading to significantly improved zero-shot and few-shot transfer performance across environments with varying visual styles, physical parameters, or non-stationary dynamics.
proposals.method: We will integrate a DANN-style architecture into a modern actor-critic framework (e.g., PPO or SAC). The agent's convolutional or transformer-based encoder will feed into two heads: a standard policy/value head, and a domain classifier head. A gradient reversal layer will be placed before the classifier, encouraging the encoder to produce features that maximize policy reward while minimizing the classifier's ability to distinguish between source domains (e.g., different visual textures in Procgen) or between segments of a non-stationary environment. We will benchmark performance on established RL transfer suites like Procgen and Meta-World, comparing against standard RL and prior domain generalization baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, empirical study of adversarial domain-invariance for RL, establishing new baselines for cross-domain generalization. It will yield a scalable method to train more robust and adaptable agents, contributing directly to the fields of transfer learning and lifelong RL. The resulting insights could inform representation learning for large, multi-task models like those in the Gemini lineage.
proposals.novelty: While DANN is well-known in supervised learning, its application to RL for learning dynamics-invariant features, rather than just visual-style invariance, is less explored. The novelty lies in the specific integration of the adversarial objective within the on-policy RL loop and its evaluation for agentic generalization, moving beyond static vision datasets.

proposals.title: Contrastive State Representations for Efficient Multi-Task and Transfer Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) is sample inefficiency, exacerbated by the need to learn task-specific representations from scratch. This severely limits transfer across tasks and domains. Pre-2026, self-supervised contrastive learning (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL) has revolutionized representation learning in vision and language by learning invariant features from augmentations, but its principled application to RL for transfer remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an RL agent with a contrastive loss on temporally-augmented state observations will yield robust, task-agnostic state representations that enable significantly faster learning and positive transfer across a distribution of related tasks, compared to standard end-to-end RL or reconstruction-based representation learning.
proposals.method: We will integrate a contrastive learning objective into an asynchronous, distributed RL training framework inspired by Rusu's work on Asynchronous Local-SGD and DiPaCo. The agent's encoder will be trained using a time-contrastive loss (e.g., across adjacent timesteps and sub-trajectories) alongside the policy gradient loss. We will employ a momentum encoder and a projection head, as in BYOL, to stabilize training. The method will be evaluated on suites of procedurally generated tasks (e.g., Procgen, Meta-World) to measure sample efficiency and zero-shot/gradient-based transfer performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable method for learning transferable RL representations without dense rewards or explicit supervision. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of contrastive objectives over reconstruction for RL transfer and release a robust benchmark for evaluating representation quality in multi-task RL settings.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established, its integration into asynchronous, distributed RL training for the explicit purpose of cross-task transfer is novel. The key insight is leveraging temporal structure within and across tasks as a natural source of augmentation for contrastive learning, moving beyond visual augmentations common in prior work.

proposals.title: DARLA-MAT: Zero-Shot Multi-Agent Transfer via Disentangled Representations of Agent-Induced Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems, a key challenge for deploying learned agents is their brittleness to new partners or opponents, whose behaviors induce a domain shift in the interactive dynamics. Pre-2026, methods like DARLA (DisentAngled Representation Learning Agent) showed promise for zero-shot visual domain adaptation in single-agent RL, but its principles have not been extended to the social, non-stationary dynamics of multi-agent environments. This limits the transferability and sample efficiency of agents in real-world collaborative or competitive settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning disentangled representations that separate an agent's own policy from latent representations of other agents' strategies and the underlying task dynamics, a trained agent can achieve robust zero-shot transfer to novel partners or opponents without additional fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will extend the DARLA framework to multi-agent settings by training a β-VAE on trajectories from diverse training partners/opponents. The model will be encouraged to disentangle a time-invariant agent-strategy vector for each co-agent from the agent's own policy and environmental state. We will test this using a distributed training paradigm inspired by DiPaCo and Asynchronous Local-SGD, training on a suite of multi-agent benchmarks (e.g., StarCraft II, Overcooked, Diplomacy) with a population of diverse partners. Transfer will be evaluated by pairing the trained agent with held-out agents exhibiting novel strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for zero-shot transfer in multi-agent RL, directly addressing the problem of non-stationarity induced by new partners. It will provide empirical benchmarks for multi-agent domain adaptation and demonstrate how disentangled representations can improve the robustness and generalizability of interactive agents.
proposals.novelty: While disentangled representation learning has been explored for single-agent domain adaptation, its principled application to model the *social* dynamics induced by other agents for zero-shot transfer is novel. This bridges the gap between representation learning for robustness and the unique challenges of multi-agent non-stationarity.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-V: Distributed Path Composition for Scalable Multi-Agent Scene Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems increasingly rely on multi-sensor or multi-agent setups to perceive complex environments, but constructing a unified, coherent scene representation from decentralized, asynchronous visual data remains a major challenge. Existing methods often require heavy synchronization or centralized fusion, which does not scale and is vulnerable to communication bottlenecks, limiting real-world deployment in robotics and embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed training framework, inspired by asynchronous and local optimization techniques, can learn robust, compositional scene representations from the inherently partial and non-IID views of multiple agents, outperforming centralized or synchronized baselines in scalability and generalization.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to the visual domain. Each agent, equipped with a local vision encoder (e.g., a ViT), will train asynchronously on its own visual stream, periodically communicating sparse updates via a shared, sparse expert mixture. We will integrate Flash Attention for efficient long-context processing of temporal visual sequences per agent. A central lightweight composer network, trained via a novel multi-view contrastive objective, will learn to fuse these asynchronous local features into a globally consistent 3D scene representation, without requiring synchronized data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel distributed algorithm for multi-view visual representation learning that is communication-efficient and robust to asynchrony, 2) a benchmark for evaluating multi-agent scene understanding under realistic decentralized constraints, and 3) empirical evidence scaling laws for performance versus number of agents, advancing towards truly scalable perceptual systems.
proposals.novelty: While DiPaCo and Local-SGD address distributed *training* of language models, their application to learning unified *geometric and semantic scene representations* from raw, asynchronous visual streams is unexplored. The key novelty is treating each agent's learning trajectory as a path to be composed, rather than forcing data centralization.

proposals.title: Temporal Generative Query Networks: Learning Disentangled Scene Dynamics from Unlabeled Video
proposals.motivation: The Generative Query Network (GQN) demonstrated powerful scene representation learning from static viewpoints, but real-world perception requires understanding how scenes evolve over time. Modeling dynamic 3D scenes from raw video sequences is a core, unsolved challenge for building generalist AI agents that can reason about and interact with a changing world. This capability is crucial for next-generation multimodal models, like those in the Gemini lineage, to move beyond static analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, factorized architecture extending the GQN can learn a disentangled representation separating persistent scene geometry from dynamic elements and their motions directly from sequences of unlabeled frames, enabling robust temporal view synthesis and future frame prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a Temporal GQN (T-GQN) with a core recurrent latent variable model. The architecture will feature separate pathways for static scene representation (using a GQN-like encoder/decoder) and dynamic latent variables modeled by a transformer or LSTM to capture temporal dependencies. We will leverage asynchronous local-SGD training principles for efficient distributed training across large-scale, multi-view video datasets (e.g., RT-X, robotics data). The model will be trained with a composite loss for view reconstruction, future frame prediction, and a regularization term encouraging disentanglement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new benchmark model for unsupervised learning of dynamic 3D scenes from video. It will provide a path composition-like framework for building complex temporal representations from simpler, distributed components. The resulting model and methodology will advance the frontier of agentic, multimodal reasoning by providing a core competency in understanding scene dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored video prediction or static neural scene representations, the explicit combination and disentanglement of static and dynamic factors within the GQN's generative framework for temporal view synthesis is novel. The integration of scalable, distributed training techniques from the DiPaCo/Async Local-SGD lineage to this problem domain is also a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: DiPaCo-RL: Composing Distributed Scene Representations for Efficient Multi-Task Robotic Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Training generalist robotic agents requires efficient learning from diverse, high-dimensional sensory data. Pre-2026, foundation models like Gemini have shown powerful scene understanding, but their representations are not optimized for sequential decision-making. Bridging this gap by using such representations as compact, semantically rich states for reinforcement learning (RL) could drastically improve sample efficiency and generalization across tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scene representation pre-trained on a large-scale multimodal corpus (e.g., using a Gemini-style encoder) and then fine-tuned via asynchronous, distributed RL algorithms will yield a more sample-efficient and transferable policy for multi-task robotic control than training from pixels or using generic visual features.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to train a scene representation encoder on diverse video and text data. This encoder will produce a fixed-dimensional state vector for an RL agent. We will then train a policy network on top of this frozen representation using an asynchronous, local-SGD variant of a state-of-the-art off-policy RL algorithm (e.g., DrQ-v2 or TD-MPC2). The training will be distributed across multiple robotic task domains (e.g., Meta-World, RLBench) to enforce representation generality, with the policy head adapted per task via a lightweight adapter.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a scalable methodology for grounding large-scale pre-trained representations in embodied control, 2) empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and transfer gains of such representations in multi-task RL benchmarks, and 3) an open-source framework combining DiPaCo-style distributed training with asynchronous RL for future research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit co-design of a distributed, compositionally trained representation (DiPaCo) with an asynchronous, distributed RL training loop, moving beyond simple feature extraction to create representations inherently shaped for efficient policy learning across a federation of tasks.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Emergent Agentic Capabilities: A Study of Model and Data Complexity in Multi-Task Transfer
proposals.motivation: The scaling of model size and dataset size has driven remarkable progress in language and multimodal models, but the relationship between scale and the emergence of complex, agentic behaviors remains poorly understood. As models like Gemini push frontiers in reasoning and long-context tasks, it is critical to systematically study how scaling both model parameters and the diversity/complexity of training data jointly influences the acquisition and transfer of these high-level capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling model size alone is insufficient for robust agentic performance; instead, a synergistic scaling of both model capacity and the compositional complexity of training tasks is required to unlock efficient, generalizable transfer to novel, complex downstream objectives.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to train a population of expert modules on a curriculum of increasingly complex, procedurally generated tasks blending reasoning, tool use, and long-context understanding. We will employ Asynchronous Local-SGD for efficient distributed training of these massive models. Performance will be evaluated on a held-out suite of agentic benchmarks, analyzing transfer efficiency, sample complexity, and the emergence of zero-shot capabilities as a function of both model scale and training-task complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled empirical framework for scaling studies beyond simple loss prediction, specifically targeting high-level capabilities. It will yield concrete scaling laws for agentic task transfer and inform the design of more efficient training curricula for next-generation AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for loss exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on scaling for emergent agentic behavior and explicitly decouples the effects of model scale from training data complexity, using a modular, population-based training approach (DiPaCo) not commonly applied to this question.

proposals.title: Amortized Variational Inference for Efficient and Robust Representation Learning in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) like Gemini rely on powerful representation networks, but their training is computationally intensive and can produce brittle or suboptimal latent spaces. Pre-2026, variational autoencoders (VAEs) offer a principled framework for learning structured representations, yet their application to billion-parameter LLMs is hindered by scalability and inference quality issues. Improving the efficiency and quality of the representation network is crucial for better sample efficiency, transfer learning, and downstream task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating advanced, scalable variational inference techniques—specifically, hierarchical latent variable models and normalizing flows—into the representation learning pipeline of large-scale models will yield more data-efficient, robust, and compositionally structured representations without sacrificing training throughput.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DiPaCo (Distributed Path Composition) framework to train a VAE-based representation network asynchronously. The encoder will employ a hierarchical latent structure, with amortized inference parameters learned via a lightweight, Flash-accelerated network. We will integrate normalizing flows into the posterior to enhance its expressiveness. Training will leverage Asynchronous Local-SGD for efficient distributed optimization across the model components, evaluating transfer performance on a suite of reasoning and multimodal tasks from the Gemini lineage.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable methodology for integrating amortized variational inference into large-scale model training. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of structured latent spaces for transfer learning and multi-task robustness in LLMs. The resulting techniques could reduce the data and compute required for fine-tuning and adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of advanced VI techniques (hierarchical VAEs, normalizing flows) within a massively distributed, asynchronous training framework (DiPaCo, Local-SGD) designed for modern LLMs, moving beyond small-scale VAE applications to billion-parameter regimes.

ideas.title: Meta-Inverse Problem: Joint Inference of Task and Adaptation Process from Parameter Dynamics
ideas.description: This approach solves the ill-posed inverse problem by inferring both the latent task description and the characteristics of the adaptation process that produced the observed parameter set. It leverages the full dynamics of parameter change during adaptation, not just the final snapshot, and uses a structured prior over a space of tasks defined by a compositional grammar. The system performs Bayesian meta-inference, comparing the observed parameter trajectory against simulations generated by a model of the learner, which results in a posterior distribution over plausible task descriptions and adaptation hyperparameters, yielding robust and interpretable inferences.

ideas.title: Inverse System Identification: Decoding Task Semantics from Parameter Perturbations
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from control theory's inverse system identification. In that field, one infers the nature of an external disturbance or command by observing how it perturbs a known system's parameters or outputs. Analogously, we propose treating a large pre-trained neural network as the known system. When the model is adapted (via fine-tuning, LoRA, etc.) to perform a new task, the change in its parameters (Δθ) is the observable perturbation. The goal is to solve the inverse problem: from Δθ, infer a latent description of the task that caused the adaptation. The adapted solution involves constructing a 'task decoder' model trained on a diverse corpus of adaptation episodes. Each episode provides a pair: the parameter delta and a representation of the task (e.g., a textual description, a program, or a latent code). The decoder learns to map the structural patterns in Δθ—which weights changed, in what direction, and by how much—back to the semantic task space. Success would enable diagnosing what a model has learned from few-shot updates, comparing task similarities geometrically in parameter-change space, and potentially guiding or regularizing future adaptations.

ideas.title: Hypernetwork Parameter Generation via Adaptive Control System Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands on using hypernetworks for parameter generation by drawing an analogy from adaptive control systems in engineering. In that field, a high-level controller continuously tunes the parameters of a lower-level subsystem based on real-time feedback, allowing for robust performance under varying conditions. Adapting this solution, we propose a hypernetwork architecture that conditions parameter generation not just on a static latent code, but also on a dynamic feedback signal from the primary network's training state (e.g., recent gradients or feature maps). This creates a meta-learning loop where the hypernetwork acts as an adaptive controller, iteratively refining the generated parameters to improve learning efficiency, convergence, and task adaptation, moving beyond one-shot conditional generation to a more responsive, closed-loop parameter synthesis system.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Meta-Learning for Many-Shot Adaptation via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'Scale to many-shot adaptation within the framework' by using an analogy from ecology. The analogous problem is how simple pioneer species facilitate the development of complex, stable ecosystems during primary succession—a parallel to scaling a few-shot learner (LEO) to data-rich environments. The analogous solution is the staged, hierarchical process of ecological community assembly. This is adapted by redesigning LEO's adaptation mechanism into a multi-phase, hierarchical meta-learning process. The model sequentially applies 'pioneer,' 'intermediate,' and 'climax' adaptation modules, each conditioned on the output of the previous stage, to gradually and robustly specialize using abundant per-task data, thereby efficiently scaling the framework's capacity.

ideas.title: Disentangled Conditional Information Bottleneck for Robust Latent Representations
ideas.description: This approach redesigns the information bottleneck for latent spaces by explicitly modeling and disentangling task-relevant and task-irrelevant information. A structured encoder produces separate latent variables, and the training objective applies a bottleneck penalty only to the irrelevant component, preventing the loss of predictive information. This conditional formulation ensures the model discards only nuisance factors while maintaining robustness across distribution shifts and adversarial perturbations. The system is evaluated on tasks requiring invariance, such as domain generalization and fair representation learning.

ideas.title: Task-Adaptive Relation Network Selection via Meta-Learning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single, fixed relation network is optimal for encoding context across all tasks. Drawing an analogy from immunology's adaptive immune system, which selects specific antibodies from a diverse repertoire to match particular pathogens, we propose a meta-learning framework for task-adaptive relation network selection. The system maintains a pre-trained repertoire of simple, specialized relation networks, each encoding different relational priors. A meta-controller, conditioned on initial task observations, selects or weights the networks in the repertoire to compose a task-specific encoder. This adapts the core relational computation to the task context, balancing flexibility and efficiency better than a one-size-fits-all architecture.

ideas.title: Apply Thermodynamic Work-Extraction Principles to the Variational Information Bottleneck
ideas.description: This idea expands the original variational information bottleneck concept by drawing an analogy from statistical thermodynamics. In thermodynamics, the optimal process for extracting work from a system balances the useful energy output against an entropic dissipation term, governed by free energy. The analogous solution involves formalisms like the Jarzynski equality. Adapted to machine learning, we treat the task-relevant information encoded in the latent space as the 'useful work' to be maximized, and the mutual information between the latent code and the input as the 'dissipation' or entropic cost to be minimized. This thermodynamic perspective leads to a novel training objective derived from fluctuation theorems, which could provide a more principled and potentially stricter regularization for the task context encoder, enforcing a sharper information bottleneck to yield more compressed and robust representations.

ideas.title: Compositional Ultra-Low-Dimensional Adaptation via Dynamic Subspace Routing
ideas.description: This research investigates adaptation via a collection of ultra-low-dimensional (2-3D) latent subspaces, not a single one. A lightweight router network dynamically selects and composes a sparse set of these minimal subspaces for each input or task, constructing a task-adapted representation. This preserves the interpretability and simplicity benefits of ultra-low-dimensional spaces while avoiding catastrophic information loss by distributing information across a compositional system. The approach enables complex adaptations by learning which minimal concepts need to be combined, effectively creating a 'vocabulary' of low-dimensional building blocks.

ideas.title: Information Bottleneck with Expressive Latent Flows for Task-Aware Compression
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) with a normalizing flow-based latent variable model. The VIB objective is applied to regularize the complex, expressive latent space defined by the flow, forcing it to discard nuisance information while preserving task-relevant features. This creates a highly flexible, non-Gaussian bottleneck that can learn compressed representations tailored to complex, real-world data structures like images or speech. The result is a model that achieves superior trade-offs between compression, robustness, and task performance compared to using either simple Gaussian priors or flow models without an information-theoretic compression objective.

ideas.title: Latent Space Refinement via Thermodynamic Annealing and Flow-Based Priors
ideas.description: This idea proposes to replace the simple Gaussian latent variable in probabilistic models with a more sophisticated prior constructed through an analogy to enhanced sampling in statistical physics. Drawing from computational materials science, where techniques like metadynamics use a time-dependent bias potential to help systems explore complex energy landscapes, we adapt this to the machine learning context. The solution is to use a normalizing flow as a base generative model for the latent space, coupled with an auxiliary network that learns an invertible 'annealing bias' applied during training. This bias actively reshapes the latent distribution, filling in low-probability regions between modes to encourage better coverage and more representative sampling, ultimately leading to a latent variable model that can capture richer and more complex data geometries.

ideas.title: Hypernetwork-Generated Task-Adaptive Relation Networks for Context Encoding
ideas.description: This system overcomes the rigidity of a fixed relation network by making its fundamental operations task-adaptive. A meta-learner ingests a few examples from a new task and produces an embedding. This embedding is used by a hypernetwork to generate the weights for the relation-scoring function and feature transformation layers within the relation module. Consequently, for a logical reasoning task, the network can learn to prioritize hierarchical relations, while for a social graph task, it can learn to emphasize bidirectional influences, all within the same overarching architecture.

ideas.title: Topological Manifold Adaptation for Ultra-Low-Dimensional Latent Spaces
ideas.description: This research investigates the extreme of using a latent space of just 2-3 dimensions for model adaptation, drawing an analogy from topology and manifold learning. In those fields, high-dimensional data with complex structure is mapped to a low-dimensional manifold by preserving only essential topological features (like connectivity) rather than precise distances. The analogous solution involves constructing a nearest-neighbor graph to capture local relationships and then embedding this graph into a minimal dimensional space. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to treat a pre-trained model's representations as samples from a high-dimensional manifold. The proposed method would first construct a sparse topological graph of the data in the original feature space to capture its essential structure. Then, it would learn an adaptation via an ultra-low-dimensional (2-3D) latent embedding that preserves this graph's connectivity, forcing all task-specific information through this severe bottleneck. The core adaptation would fine-tune only the projection into this minimal space and a simple decoder, radically reducing parameters and potentially yielding highly interpretable 2D/3D latent representations while maintaining performance through preserved topological integrity.

ideas.title: Amortized Proposals with Local Iterative Refinement for Robust Variational Inference
ideas.description: This system mitigates the amortization gap by not relying solely on a fixed inference network. It employs a two-stage process: first, a lightweight amortized network provides an initial posterior estimate and a proposal distribution. Second, for each new data point, a small number of gradient-based updates are performed, using the original generative model to tailor the posterior approximation locally. This ensures robust performance even on data that diverges from the training distribution, yielding more accurate uncertainty quantification without sacrificing the core efficiency of amortization.

ideas.title: Adapting Genetic Regulatory Networks for Neural Parameter Generation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes investigating the impact of different neural network decoders for mapping latent codes to parameters. Through analogical reasoning with developmental biology, we adapt the solution of genetic regulatory networks. In Evo-Devo, a compact genome is interpreted by a complex regulatory system to generate specific phenotypic parameters. We adapt this by designing novel decoder architectures where the latent code modulates a dynamic, modular computational network—akin to transcription factors regulating gene expression. This approach moves beyond dense, monolithic decoders to explore sparse, hierarchical, and conditionally-activated pathways for parameter generation, potentially leading to more efficient, interpretable, and robust models for tasks like hypernetwork design or neural architecture search.

ideas.title: Adapt compiler optimization caching for amortized variational inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from compiler design to improve amortized variational inference in Bayesian deep learning. In JIT compilation, expensive optimization is amortized by caching compiled code for frequent execution paths. We propose an analogous 'speculative inference cache' system. The amortized inference network would be augmented to identify and cache posterior parameters for common latent patterns or input features. During inference, the system speculatively matches new data to cached patterns, using the cached posterior if confidence is high, and falls back to the full network otherwise. The cache is dynamically updated based on the observed frequency of patterns, ensuring computational cost is amortized most effectively over the true data distribution, similar to profile-guided optimization in compilers.

ideas.title: Dynamic Latent-Conditioned Parameter Modulation via Runtime Context
ideas.description: This approach introduces a recurrent modulation network that operates in tandem with the primary model. During each forward pass, the modulator observes a summary of the primary model's current internal state and, conditioned on this context and the original latent code, generates a delta update to a subset of the primary model's parameters. This creates a closed-loop system where the generated parameters are not static but dynamically adapted to the immediate computational context, enabling finer-grained control and improved robustness to input variations. The system is trained end-to-end with a stability constraint to ensure the iterative modifications do not cause chaotic behavior.

ideas.title: Latent Space NAS via Molecular Docking Simulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational drug discovery to the problem of few-shot neural architecture search (NAS). The analogous field is Computational Drug Discovery, where the standard solution is molecular docking simulation. This involves searching a vast space of potential drug molecules by simulating how well each molecule (a ligand) fits into a target protein's binding site, predicting binding affinity without synthesizing every candidate. We adapt this by conceptualizing the latent space of a generative model trained on neural architectures as the 'binding pocket'. Candidate architectures, encoded as latent vectors, are the 'ligands'. A surrogate performance predictor, acting as the docking scoring function, is trained on a few-shot dataset of (latent vector, validation performance) pairs. The search then optimizes within the continuous latent space for vectors predicted to yield high-performance architectures, dramatically reducing the number of architectures that need to be fully trained and evaluated.

ideas.title: Structured, Non-Gaussian Latent Processes for Generating Neural Network Parameters
ideas.description: This research direction challenges both the simplistic Gaussian prior over latent variables and the independent generation of network parameters. It proposes using a structured, potentially non-Gaussian stochastic process (e.g., a Bayesian neural network or a diffusion process) as the latent generator, where the process's structure mirrors the computational graph of the target network. This allows the decoder to generate parameters not as independent draws, but as correlated samples from a distribution whose complexity (e.g., multimodality, heavy tails) and inter-parameter dependencies are informed by the network's architecture and intended function. The result is a more faithful and compact representation of uncertainty that propagates through the network's layers, capturing higher-order statistical relationships between weights that are crucial for robust predictions and reliable uncertainty estimates.

ideas.title: Structural Hypernetworks from Ultra-Low-Dimensional Latent Codes
ideas.description: This research investigates a hypernetwork that generates all parameters of a target network from a latent code of only 2 or 3 dimensions. The key innovation is that this ultra-low-dimensional code does not generate parameters independently; instead, it is forced to generate them through a process that inherently respects and discovers the fundamental computational structure and weight interdependencies of the target network. This creates a continuous, human-interpretable latent space where each point corresponds to a complete, functional neural network with coherent internal structure. The result is a powerful tool for visualizing and navigating the space of viable network architectures and their parameters, enabling new forms of model adaptation, compression, and architectural search through simple operations in a minimal latent space.

ideas.title: Applying Hierarchical Protein Folding Principles to Meta-Learn Billion-Parameter Transformers
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical solution from protein folding to scale LEO to meta-learn transformers with billions of parameters. In computational biology, the intractable problem of predicting a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence is solved through a hierarchical process: local structural motifs (alpha-helices, beta-sheets) are predicted first and then assembled into global domains and full structures. Analogously, we propose to meta-learn not the full high-dimensional parameter vector directly, but a compact set of 'architectural blueprints' that define functional sub-modules and their interaction rules. LEO would operate on this compressed, semantically structured space. The final billion-parameter model is then generated by a deterministic, differentiable 'folding' function that instantiates the parameters according to the meta-learned blueprints, making the optimization tractable and inherently biased towards learning modular, composable, and scalable neural architectures.

ideas.title: Adaptively Sparse Normalizing Flows with Complexity Gating for Efficient Latent Modeling
ideas.description: This approach introduces an efficiency-aware framework for sophisticated latent distributions. Instead of applying a full normalizing flow transformation to the entire latent vector for every datapoint, the system learns to route information through either a simple Gaussian path or a more expressive flow-based path. The routing is conditioned on the input and uses a sparsity-promoting penalty to ensure the complex path is used sparingly but effectively. This results in a model that retains the representational power for complex data manifolds while maintaining near-Gaussian efficiency for simpler, more isotropic regions of the latent space.

ideas.title: Symbolic Neural Architecture Search (S-NAS): Discovering Interpretable Building Blocks via Latent Space Regression
ideas.description: This research direction fuses symbolic regression and latent-space Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to create Symbolic NAS (S-NAS). The core idea is to use a variational autoencoder to embed neural network architectures into a continuous latent space, where a performance predictor guides an evolutionary search for high-performing latent codes. The key innovation is applying symbolic regression to the trajectories and clusters of high-performing codes in this latent space to distill the search results into compact, interpretable symbolic formulas. These formulas explicitly describe the functional relationships between architectural hyperparameters (e.g., layer depth, width, connectivity) and performance, yielding both an optimized architecture and a general, interpretable rule for what makes architectures effective for a given task.

ideas.title: Diagnosing Latent Optimization Failure Modes via Expressive, Learnable Posterior Manifolds
ideas.description: This research investigates the failure conditions of low-dimensional latent optimization by replacing simple Gaussian priors with sophisticated, learnable probabilistic models such as normalizing flows or mixture distributions. The core synergy is that the complexity of the latent distribution itself becomes a tool for diagnosis: we can analyze how properties like multi-modality, curvature, and topological constraints of the learned latent manifold correlate with optimization pathology. This allows us to move beyond the question of 'when' latent optimization fails to a mechanistic understanding of 'why,' based on the mismatch between the optimization landscape in latent space and the true parameter space. The outcome is a set of principled guidelines for designing latent spaces and optimization procedures that are robust to these identified failure modes.

ideas.title: Decoder Generates Network Parameters via Crystallographic Symmetry Rules
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a decoder must generate all network parameters independently by drawing an analogy from crystallography. In that field, the immense, repeating structure of a crystal is not enumerated atom-by-atom but is compactly represented by an asymmetric unit and symmetry operations. Adapting this solution, the research proposes that a decoder should not output raw parameters but should instead generate a compact seed of core parameters and a set of learned 'symmetry' rules—such as weight-sharing patterns, transformation templates, or architectural constraints—that algorithmically expand the seed into the complete network. This approach explicitly leverages the known structural priors of neural networks (like translational invariance in CNNs or permutation symmetries) to make the generation process more efficient and structurally coherent.

ideas.title: Diagnosing and Remedying Latent Non-Gaussianity with Mixture Models and Task-Aware Validation
ideas.description: This research program first establishes a concrete, quantifiable failure mode of the Gaussian latent assumption by developing a diagnostic suite that measures its inadequacy in capturing multi-modality, skew, and heavy tails in Bayesian neural networks or variational autoencoders. It then proposes a scalable, flexible alternative using a sparse mixture of Gaussian distributions, where the number of components is regulated via a sparsity-inducing prior to avoid overfitting. Finally, the approach validates the improvement not just in density estimation, but by demonstrating enhanced robustness and calibrated uncertainty in downstream decision-making tasks, providing a clear cost-benefit analysis for moving beyond the simple Gaussian.

ideas.description: This approach reframes network generation as the generation of a structured blueprint and a set of adaptive modulations. A decoder hypernetwork outputs a compact specification defining layer types, connectivity graphs, and weight-sharing groups, alongside modulation vectors. These outputs are applied to a foundational, meta-trained network substrate whose core parameters are shared and static. The system thus constructs a wide variety of effective architectures by composing known neural components and sparsely adapting them, inheriting strong inductive biases while maintaining flexibility.

ideas.description: This approach first trains a model with a strong disentanglement objective or a concept bottleneck layer to force the latent representation to factorize into semantically meaningful and independent variables. Symbolic regression is then not applied to the raw, high-dimensional latent code, but to the low-dimensional, human-named concept variables, searching for parsimonious mathematical relationships that predict model decisions or other concepts. The system validates the discovered symbolic expressions by testing their predictive power and consistency on data from shifted distributions, ensuring they capture fundamental causal or conceptual relationships rather than dataset-specific correlations.

ideas.title: Bayesian Mixture Models for Latent Parameter Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research challenges the standard assumption that a simple Gaussian suffices to capture all latent parameter uncertainty in models. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where the uncertainty in complex, episodic phenomena like earthquake recurrence is modeled using Bayesian mixture models to capture multi-modality and heavy tails, we propose adapting this solution. The adapted idea is to replace the standard Gaussian latent variable with a flexible Bayesian mixture model (e.g., a Gaussian Scale Mixture or a nonparametric Dirichlet Process mixture). This allows the model's uncertainty representation to capture more complex structures—such as multiple plausible parameter regimes, skewness, and fat tails—leading to more robust uncertainty quantification, better-calibrated predictions, and improved handling of model misspecification and out-of-distribution data.

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Parsimony Methods to Latent Space Traversal
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from evolutionary biology—parsimony-based tree inference—to the problem of finding interpretable structures in latent spaces. In phylogenetics, parsimony algorithms find the evolutionary tree requiring the fewest mutations to explain observed genetic data, favoring simple, interpretable models. Adapted here, a search algorithm would treat a latent space traversal (e.g., a path changing an attribute like 'smiling') as a sequence of 'states.' Using symbolic regression as the engine to propose transformation rules, the method would enforce a parsimony constraint to find the simplest symbolic expression (e.g., 'z_new = z_old + 0.7*v_smile') that accurately reconstructs the traversal. This yields an interpretable, formulaic description of latent directions, moving beyond dense, uninterpretable vectors.
